elements.sent-2411-001: Many statistical applications, such as the Principal Component Analysis, matrix completion, tensor regression and many others, rely on accurate estimation of leading eigenvectors of a matrix.
elements.sent-2411-002: The Davis-Khan theorem is known to be instrumental for bounding above the distances between matrices $U$ and $\widehat{U}$ of population eigenvectors and their sample versions.
elements.sent-2411-003: While those distances can be measured in various metrics, the recent developments showed advantages of evaluation of the deviation in the 2-to-infinity norm.
elements.sent-2411-004: The purpose of this paper is to provide upper bounds for the distances between $U$ and $\hU$ in the two-to-infinity norm for a variety of possible scenarios and competitive approaches.
elements.sent-2411-005: Although this problem has been studied by several authors, the difference between this paper and its predecessors is that the upper bounds are obtained with no or mild probabilistic assumptions on the error distributions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Those bounds are subsequently refined, when some generic probabilistic assumptions on the errors hold.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, the paper provides alternative methods for evaluation of $\hU$ and, therefore, enables one to compare the resulting accuracies.

elements.sent-2411-053: We develop upper bounds for $\|\hU - U W_U\|\tinf$ with no additional assumptions, when $U$ and $\hU$ are obtained from either a symmetric or non-symmetric matrix.
elements.sent-2411-054: Although those upper bounds sometimes involve a number of quantiles, they are completely straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the case when the data and the error matrices are not symmetric, we show that symmetrizing the problem often leads to more accurate upper bounds for $\|\hU - U W_U\|\tinf$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Although the main objective of the paper is to establish upper bounds for $\|\hU - U W_U\|\tinf$ that are valid for any errors, generic results are supplemented by the upper bounds, derived under mild probabilistic assumptions on the error matrices.

elements.section-2411-005: Perfect clustering using the two-to-infinity norm and its applications to random networks.
elements.sent-2411-188: In the last decade, evaluation of accuracy of clustering techniques came to the frontier of the statistical science.
elements.sent-2411-189: Recently a number of papers studied precision of the k-means algorithm (or its versions, like k-medoids).
elements.sent-2411-190: Since data are usually contaminated by noise, it needs to be pre-processed prior to using the k-means algorithm (\citep{giraud2018partial , \citep{H_Zhou_AOS2021_Spec_Clust ).}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Therefore, various techniques for pre-processing data were developed, such as Semidefinite Programming (SDP) (\citep{giraud2018partial , \citep{NIPS2017_6776 ), or spectral analysis (\citep{abbe_fan_AOS2022 , \citep{H_Zhou_AOS2021_Spec_Clust , \citep{ndaoud_aos2022 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: In particular, it turns out that spectral methods in combination with k-means/medoid clustering algorithms produce very accurate clustering assignments in a variety of problems, from Gaussian mixture models to random networks (\citep{abbe_fan_AOS2022 , \citep{lei2021biasadjusted , \citep{lei2015 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Theoretical assessments of clustering precision rely on various error metrics.
elements.sent-2411-194: For example, \citep{giraud2018partial and \citep{NIPS2017_6776 use the $l_1$-norm of the difference between the membership matrix and its SDP-based estimator for derivation of the clustering precision.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: The accuracy of approaches that use variants of the SVD are usually based on the operational norm of the induced errors (\citep{lei2021biasadjusted , \citep{H_Zhou_AOS2021_Spec_Clust ).}}
elements.sent-2411-196: While this is totally justifiable in the case when the original errors are Gaussian or sub-gaussian, as it happens in the above cited papers, in the situations where the distributions of errors are arbitrary, it is sometimes very difficult to construct tight upper bounds for the operational norm.
elements.sent-2411-197: Consider a version of the k-means setting, where rows of matrix $X \in \RR^{n \times m}$ take $r$ different values $\Te(k,:)$, $k \in [r]$.
elements.sent-2411-198: Hence, there exists a clustering function $z: [n] \to [r]$ such that $X(i,:) = \Te(z(i), :)$, $i \in [n]$.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this case, $X$ can be presented $X = Z \Te$, where $\Te \in \RR^{r \times m}$ and $Z \in \{0,1\}^{n \times r}$ is a clustering matrix, such that $Z(i,k) = 1$ if $z(i) = k$, and $Z(i,k) = 0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-200: In this scenario, data come in the form of $\hX \in \RR^{n \times m}$, and the goal is to estimate the clustering function $z$.
elements.sent-2411-201: In what follows, we denote the size of the $k$-th cluster by $n_k$, $\nmax = \displaystyle \max_k n_k$ and $\nmin = \displaystyle \min_k n_k$.
elements.sent-2411-202: Since clustering is unique only up to a permutation of cluster labels, denote the set of $r$-dimensional permutation functions of $[r]$ by $\aleph(r)$.
elements.sent-2411-203: For simplicity, let $r$ be known, and let $\hz:[n] \to [r]$ be an estimated clustering assignment.
elements.sent-2411-204: The number of errors of a clustering assignment $\hz$ with respect to the true clustering function $z$, and the associated error rate are then defined, respectively, as \be \calN_n (\hz, z) = \min_{\phi \in \aleph(r)}\ \sum_{l=1 ^n I \lkr \phi(\hz(i) \rkr \neq z(i)), \quad \calR_n (\hz, z) = n^{-1}\, \calN (\hz, z).}
elements.sent-2411-205: If $\calN_n (\hz, z) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, then clustering is called {\it strongly consistent .}
elements.sent-2411-206: In the case of a strongly consistent clustering algorithm, for $n$ large enough, one obtains $\calN_n <1$, which is equivalent to $\calN_n =0$.
elements.sent-2411-207: In this case, $\hz = \phi(z)$ for some $\phi \in \aleph(r)$, and one achieves {\it perfect clustering .}
elements.sent-2411-208: It turns out that application of two-to-infinity norm allows to establish conditions for strongly consistent clustering under rather generic assumptions.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Perfect clustering in a sub-sampled network
elements.sent-2411-281: Consider a binary undirected stochastic network on $n$ nodes, that can be partitioned into $r$ communities.
elements.sent-2411-282: Let $z:[n] \to [r]$ be a clustering function, such that $z(i) = k$ if node $i$ belongs to community $k$.
elements.sent-2411-283: Additionally, assume that the network is equipped with the Stochastic Block Model ({\bf SBM ) \citep{JMLR:v18:16-480 , so that there exists a matrix $Q \in [0,1]^{r \times r}$ of block connection probabilities, such that the probability of connection between nodes $i$ and $j$ is fully determined by the communities to which they belong: $P(i,j) = Q(z(i), z(j))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-284: In this setting, one observes an adjacency matrix $A \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}$ where, for $1 \leq i < j \leq n$, elements $A(i,j)$ of $A$ are independent Bernoulli variables with $\PP \lfi A(i,j) = 1 \rfi = P(i,j)$.
elements.sent-2411-286: Since usually networks are sparse, i.e., probabilities of connections become smaller as the network size $n$ grows, introduce a sparsity factor $\rhon$, where $\rhon$ is defined as follows: \bes P = \rhon\, P_0 \quad \mbox{with \quad \|P_0\|_{\infty = 1, \quad Q = \rhon\, Q_0 \quad with \quad P_0(i,j) = Q_0(z(i), z(j)), \ees and $\rhon = o(1)$ as $n \to \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: The main question of interest in this setting is recovery of the community assignment $z$.
elements.sent-2411-288: The problem of community detection in the SBM was addressed in an abundance of publications, under a variety of assumptions (see, e.g., \citep{JMLR:v18:16-480 , \citep{abbe_bandeira_2016 , \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/AminiL14 , \citep{rohe2011spectral , \citep{Ander_Zhang_IEEE2024_SBM among others).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: At present, perhaps the most popular method of community detection is spectral clustering that was studied in, e.g., \citep{lei2015 and \citep{rohe2011spectral .}}
elements.sent-2411-290: However, this procedure becomes prohibitively computationally expensive when the number of nodes is huge.
elements.sent-2411-291: For this reason, recently several authors suggested a variety of approaches for reduction of computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-292: Majority of those proposals start with sub-sampling a group of nodes, and then partitioning those nodes into communities.
elements.sent-2411-293: This process may be repeated several times in order to obtain community assignment of all nodes, as in, e.g., \citep{chakrabarty2023sonnet and \citep{mukherjee2021two , In this section, we address the first part of this process: sub-sampling of nodes with the subsequent community assignment.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: We would like to remind a reader that our goal here is to formulate sufficient conditions for strongly consistent clustering in a sub-sampled network.
elements.sent-2411-295: As such, we are not interested in assessment of a sharp threshold for possibility of community detection, as it is done in, e.g., \citep{JMLR:v18:16-480 , \citep{abbe_bandeira_2016 or \citep{Ander_Zhang_IEEE2024_SBM , under the assumption that the connection probabilities take only two distinct values.}}}
elements.sent-2411-296: Instead, we would like to provide a practitioner with a tool for evaluation, how the sample size should be chosen under generic regularity conditions.
elements.sent-2411-297: In what follows, we assume that a group $\calS$ of $m$ nodes is sampled uniformly at random.
elements.sent-2411-298: Denote by $\Sc$ the set of remaining nodes.
elements.sent-2411-299: The goal here is to estimate community assignments of the $m$ nodes in $\calS$.
elements.sent-2411-300: It appears that many papers estimate community assignment on the basis of solely the $(m \times m)$ portion $\ASS \in \{0,1\}^{m \times m}$ of matrix $A$, as it is done in, e.g., \citep{chakrabarty2023sonnet or \citep{mukherjee2021two .}}

elements.sent-2411-303: Then, it is well known that, if $m\, \rhon$ is bounded above by a constant, then community assignment is inconsistent, while $m\, \rhon > C \log n$, for a sufficiently large constant $C$, leads to perfect clustering of $m$ nodes into communities.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Perfect clustering of layers in a diverse multilayer network
